Template version: 2026-06 · PostgreSQL 18 · Patroni 3.3 · etcd 3.5

docs.title: Replication Slots — Management & Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Replication slots guarantee WAL retention for standbys, logical subscribers, and backup tools. Misconfigured slots are a top cause of disk full on PostgreSQL primaries.

PostgreSQL 18 — physical and logical slots. See Streaming Replication · Logical Replication

docs.title: Slot Types
docs.text: Type Created by Purpose Physical pg_create_physical_replication_slot, pg_basebackup -S, Patroni Streaming standby WAL stream Logical pg_create_logical_replication_slot, SUBSCRIPTION Logical decoding / CDC

docs.title: View All Slots
docs.text:
SELECT slot_name, slot_type, active, temporary, database,
       restart_lsn, confirmed_flush_lsn,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) AS retained_bytes,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS retained
FROM pg_replication_slots
ORDER BY pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) DESC NULLS LAST;
Column Meaning active Consumer currently connected temporary Dropped when session ends (pg_basebackup -C) restart_lsn Earliest WAL still needed confirmed_flush_lsn Logical: last confirmed by consumer

docs.title: Create Physical Slot
docs.text:
SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('standby1');
-- Idempotent PG 17+:
SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('standby1', true, false);

Use with standby:

primary_slot_name = 'standby1'

Or pg_basebackup:

pg_basebackup ... -S standby1
# Temporary slot during backup:
pg_basebackup ... -C -S tmp_backup_slot

docs.title: Create Logical Slot
docs.text:
SELECT pg_create_logical_replication_slot('app_sub_slot', 'pgoutput');

Usually created automatically by CREATE SUBSCRIPTION ... WITH (create_slot = true).

docs.title: Drop Slot (Free WAL)
docs.text:
-- Confirm inactive and consumer gone
SELECT * FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'old_standby';

SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('old_standby');

Warning: Dropping an active slot breaks the connected replica/subscriber.

docs.title: Inactive Slot — Disk Fill Scenario
docs.text:
Symptom: pg_wal/ growing, disk full, archive OK
Cause:   inactive slot with old restart_lsn — WAL cannot be recycled

-- Find culprit
SELECT slot_name, active,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS wal_held
FROM pg_replication_slots
WHERE NOT active AND pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) > 1073741824;

Fix:

  1. If replica abandoned → pg_drop_replication_slot
  2. If replica should exist → rebuild standby or fix connectivity
  3. Never delete WAL files manually while slots exist

docs.title: max_slot_wal_keep_size (PG 13+)
docs.text:
Limits WAL retained for inactive slots — slot becomes invalid if exceeded.

max_slot_wal_keep_size = 4GB   # or -1 unlimited (default pre-PG 13 behavior via wal_keep)

Monitor invalidation:

SELECT slot_name, active, invalidation_reason
FROM pg_replication_slots;   -- PG 17+ invalidation_reason

After invalidation, replica needs rebuild (pg_basebackup / pgBackRest).

docs.title: Patroni Slots
docs.text:
Patroni creates and manages patroni slots on replicas. Do not drop manually during normal operation.

patronictl list
patronictl show-config

docs.title: Logical Slot Lag
docs.text:
SELECT slot_name, active,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), confirmed_flush_lsn)) AS lag
FROM pg_replication_slots
WHERE slot_type = 'logical';

High lag → subscriber down or slow; WAL accumulates on primary.

Fix: Start subscriber, or drop subscription/slot if abandoned.

docs.title: Monitoring Alerts
Best Practices
docs.text:
• One permanent physical slot per standby
  • Name slots clearly (standby1, dc2_dr, not slot1)
  • Drop slots when decommissioning replicas
  • Monitor weekly in DBA checklist
  • Set max_slot_wal_keep_size to bound runaway retention
  • Document slots in infrastructure inventory

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Streaming Replication
  • Logical Replication
  • pg_basebackup
  • DBA Health Checks
  • Common Errors — disk full

docs.title: Streaming (Physical) Replication
docs.text:
PostgreSQL 18 — standard streaming replication; use scram-sha-256 for replication users. HA setup: PG 18 HA Runbook.

Compare with logical replication: Logical vs Physical · Logical Replication

Binary replication of WAL from primary to one or more standby servers. Standbys are read-only (hot standby).

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
# Primary postgresql.conf
wal_level = replica
max_wal_senders = 10
max_replication_slots = 10
wal_keep_size = 1GB
archive_mode = on   # recommended for PITR

CREATE ROLE replicator LOGIN REPLICATION PASSWORD '...';
SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('standby1');

# pg_hba.conf on primary
hostssl replication replicator 10.0.2.0/24 scram-sha-256

docs.title: Standby Setup (pg_basebackup)
docs.text:
Full guide: pg_basebackup

pg_basebackup -h primary -U replicator -D $PGDATA -Fp -Xs -P -R

Edit postgresql.auto.conf:

primary_conninfo = 'host=primary port=5432 user=replicator password=... sslmode=require'
primary_slot_name = 'standby1'

touch $PGDATA/standby.signal   # created by -R in PG 12+
pg_ctl -D $PGDATA start

docs.title: Monitor Replication
docs.text:
On primary:

SELECT client_addr, state, sent_lsn, write_lsn, flush_lsn, replay_lsn,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(sent_lsn, replay_lsn) AS replay_lag_bytes
FROM pg_stat_replication;

SELECT slot_name, active, pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) AS retained_bytes
FROM pg_replication_slots;

On standby:

SELECT pg_last_wal_receive_lsn(), pg_last_wal_replay_lsn(),
       pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp(),
       now() - pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS replay_delay;

docs.title: Synchronous Replication
docs.text:
synchronous_commit = on
synchronous_standby_names = 'FIRST 1 (standby1, standby2)'
# or: 'ANY 2 (standby1, standby2, standby3)'

-- Standby application_name must match
ALTER SYSTEM SET synchronous_standby_names = 'FIRST 1 (standby1)';
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

Trade-off: zero data loss on primary failure vs. write latency.

docs.title: Replication Slots
docs.text:
Prevent WAL removal until consumer replays:

SELECT * FROM pg_replication_slots;

-- Danger: inactive slot causes WAL bloat on primary
-- Monitor pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)

docs.title: Cascading Replication
docs.text:
Standby can stream to downstream standbys:

# intermediate standby
max_wal_senders = 10
hot_standby = on

docs.title: Hot Standby Conflicts
docs.text:
Long queries on standby can block WAL replay:

-- Standby
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_database_conflicts;

-- Mitigation on standby
hot_standby_feedback = on
max_standby_streaming_delay = 30s

Or cancel conflicting queries on standby.

docs.title: Read-Only Queries on Standby
docs.text:
-- Default: standbys accept reads
hot_standby = on

Use connection routing (PgPool, HAProxy, app config) to send SELECTs to replicas.

docs.title: WAL Shipping vs. Streaming
docs.text:
Method Transport Lag Streaming TCP walsender/walreceiver Seconds Archive + restore archive_command + restore_command Minutes

Streaming is standard for HA; archiving adds PITR capability.

See Replication Slots for inactive slot disk fill.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Replication Slots
  • Logical Replication
  • Failover
  • Physical Backup

docs.title: In-Memory Features & Integration
docs.text:
PostgreSQL 18 is a disk-first database — it does not ship an Oracle-style In-Memory Column Store. This guide covers native RAM features, tuning, warm-cache tools, and integration with external in-memory systems (Redis, etc.).

See also: Architecture — Memory · Tuning Parameters · PG 18 AIO

docs.title: Memory Model Overview
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     SHARED MEMORY (all backends)                  │
│  shared_buffers │ WAL buffers │ lock tables │ proc array │ clog  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▲                    ▲
         │                    │
┌────────┴────────┐  ┌────────┴────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐
│ Backend 1       │  │ Backend 2       │  │ Autovacuum worker   │
│ work_mem        │  │ work_mem        │  │ maintenance_work_mem│
│ temp_buffers    │  │ temp_buffers    │  │                     │
│ catalog cache   │  │ catalog cache   │  │                     │
└─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘
         ▲
┌────────┴────────┐
│ OS Page Cache   │  ← effective_cache_size hints planner; PG + OS both cache pages
└─────────────────┘
Region Scope Survives disconnect? shared_buffers Cluster Yes work_mem Per operation per backend No temp_buffers Per session No OS page cache Server Yes (until evicted) UNLOGGED tables On disk, minimal WAL Until crash/restart

docs.title: 1. shared_buffers — Primary Page Cache
docs.text:
PostgreSQL's main buffer pool for table and index pages (default 8 KB pages).

# postgresql.conf — PG 18 production starting point (32 GB RAM server)
shared_buffers = 8GB              # ~25% RAM; cap 8–16 GB often optimal on Linux

SHOW shared_buffers;
SELECT setting, unit FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'shared_buffers';

-- Buffer hit ratio (target > 99% OLTP)
SELECT
  sum(blks_hit) * 100.0 / nullif(sum(blks_hit) + sum(blks_read), 0) AS cache_hit_pct
FROM pg_stat_database;

Integration note: Larger shared_buffers is not always faster — OS cache also holds pages. On Linux, 25% RAM is the usual starting point.

docs.title: 2. effective_cache_size — Planner Hint (Not Allocated Memory)
docs.text:
Tells the optimizer how much RAM is available for caching (PG + OS).

effective_cache_size = 24GB    # ~50–75% of total RAM on dedicated DB server

Does not allocate memory — only affects index vs sequential scan cost estimates.

docs.title: 3. work_mem — In-Memory Sorts & Hashes
docs.text:
Each sort, hash join, or merge operation can use up to work_mem per node per query.

work_mem = 32MB    # global default — keep conservative

-- Reporting role gets more
ALTER ROLE analyst SET work_mem = '256MB';

-- Single heavy query
SET work_mem = '512MB';
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ... ORDER BY ...;
RESET work_mem;

Danger: work_mem × active sorts × connections can exhaust RAM.

Safe default ≈ (RAM - shared_buffers) / (max_connections × 3)

Watch for disk spills:

-- Queries spilling to disk
SELECT query, calls, temp_blks_written
FROM pg_stat_statements
WHERE temp_blks_written > 0
ORDER BY temp_blks_written DESC LIMIT 10;

docs.title: 4. maintenance_work_mem — VACUUM, CREATE INDEX, ALTER
docs.text:
maintenance_work_mem = 1GB     # 5–10% RAM; cap ~2 GB unless bulk maintenance

-- Single large index build
SET maintenance_work_mem = '2GB';
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_big ON large_table (col);

Used by: VACUUM, CREATE INDEX, ALTER TABLE ADD FOREIGN KEY, parallel vacuum workers.

docs.title: 5. temp_buffers — Session Temp Tables
docs.text:
temp_buffers = 32MB    # per session; only for TEMP tables

SET temp_buffers = '256MB';
CREATE TEMP TABLE staging AS SELECT * FROM orders WHERE ...;

Memory returned at session end.

docs.title: 6. hash_mem_multiplier (PG 13+)
docs.text:
Scales hash table memory relative to work_mem for hash joins/aggs.

hash_mem_multiplier = 2.0    # hash operations can use 2× work_mem

docs.title: 7. Huge Pages (Linux) — Shared Memory Integration
docs.text:
Reduces TLB misses for large shared_buffers.

# Estimate pages (2MB huge pages)
echo $(($(grep shared_buffers /path/postgresql.conf | awk '{print $3}') * 8192 / 2 / 1024 / 1024))

# /etc/sysctl.d
vm.nr_hugepages = 4096

huge_pages = try    # or on — fail start if unavailable when 'on'

SELECT * FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'huge_pages';

Verify:

grep HugePages /proc/meminfo

docs.title: 8. pg_prewarm — Warm shared_buffers After Restart
docs.text:
Loads relation pages into shared_buffers from disk — reduces cold-cache latency.

CREATE EXTENSION pg_prewarm;

-- Warm entire table
SELECT pg_prewarm('orders');

-- Options: 'buffer' (shared_buffers), 'read' (OS cache only), 'prefetch'
SELECT pg_prewarm('orders', 'buffer');

-- Autoprewarm — save/load buffer state across restarts (PG 18: check extension docs)
-- shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_prewarm'
-- pg_prewarm.autoprewarm = on

HA failover: Run pg_prewarm on new primary after promotion for hot tables.

-- Warm top tables script
SELECT pg_prewarm(c.oid::regclass)
FROM pg_class c
JOIN pg_stat_user_tables s ON s.relid = c.oid
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(c.oid) DESC
LIMIT 20;

docs.title: 9. pg_buffercache — Inspect What Is in RAM
docs.text:
CREATE EXTENSION pg_buffercache;

-- Pages cached per table
SELECT c.relname,
       count(*) AS buffers,
       pg_size_pretty(count(*) * 8192) AS cached
FROM pg_buffercache b
JOIN pg_class c ON b.relfilenode = c.relfilenode
WHERE c.relname = 'orders'
GROUP BY c.relname;

-- Cache usage summary
SELECT usagecount, count(*) FROM pg_buffercache GROUP BY usagecount;

docs.title: 10. UNLOGGED Tables — Minimal WAL (Not True In-Memory)
docs.text:
Data on disk but not crash-safe — contents lost on unclean shutdown. Faster writes (no WAL for data).

CREATE UNLOGGED TABLE session_cache (
  session_id uuid PRIMARY KEY,
  payload jsonb,
  expires_at timestamptz
);

-- Revert to logged
ALTER TABLE session_cache SET LOGGED;   -- triggers full rewrite
Use Avoid Staging ETL Financial data Session scratch Anything needed after crash Rebuildable cache Replication to standby (UNLOGGED not replicated)

docs.title: 11. MATERIALIZED VIEW — Disk-Backed Result Cache
docs.text:
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_daily_sales AS
  SELECT date_trunc('day', created_at) AS day, sum(total) AS revenue
  FROM orders GROUP BY 1;

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON mv_daily_sales (day);

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY mv_daily_sales;

Schedule refresh via pg_cron.

docs.title: 12. PostgreSQL 18 — Async I/O & Memory
docs.text:
AIO improves read throughput — works with shared_buffers and OS cache, not a separate memory store.

io_method = worker
io_combine_limit = 128kB
effective_io_concurrency = 200

SELECT * FROM pg_aios;

docs.title: Memory Parameters — Complete Reference
docs.text:
Parameter Default (approx) Scope Restart? shared_buffers 128MB Cluster Yes effective_cache_size 4GB Planner hint No work_mem 4MB Per sort/hash op No maintenance_work_mem 64MB Per maintenance op No temp_buffers 8MB Per session No wal_buffers auto Cluster Yes hash_mem_multiplier 2.0 Per hash op No logical_decoding_work_mem 64MB Logical replication No max_stack_depth 2MB Per backend No shared_memory_size — PG 18: total shared mem — shared_memory_size_in_huge_pages — PG 18: huge page count — 
-- PG 18: inspect shared memory allocation
SELECT name, setting, unit, context
FROM pg_settings
WHERE name LIKE '%mem%' OR name LIKE '%buffer%'
ORDER BY name;

docs.title: sysctl tuning
docs.text:
# /etc/sysctl.d/99-postgresql-memory.conf
vm.swappiness = 1                 # avoid swapping shared_buffers
vm.overcommit_memory = 2          # strict overcommit
vm.dirty_background_ratio = 5
vm.dirty_ratio = 10

sudo sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-postgresql-memory.conf

docs.title: Transparent Huge Pages (THP)
docs.text:
PostgreSQL recommends disabling THP on Linux — can cause latency spikes.

echo never | sudo tee /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
echo never | sudo tee /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag

Use explicit huge_pages = try/on instead.

docs.title: Swap
docs.text:
Avoid swap for DB servers. If unavoidable:

# Monitor swap usage
free -h
vmstat 1

-- OOM risk — find long-running heavy queries
SELECT pid, usename, state, left(query, 80) AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'active' AND pid <> pg_backend_pid();

-- PG 18: per-backend memory (if enabled)
SELECT pid, memory_context_name, total_bytes
FROM pg_backend_memory_contexts
WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid()
ORDER BY total_bytes DESC LIMIT 20;

docs.title: External In-Memory Integration Patterns
docs.text: PostgreSQL has no built-in Redis/Memcached module in core. Integrate at application or extension layer.

docs.title: Pattern A — Application Cache (Most Common)
docs.text:
App ──► Redis (hot keys, sessions, rate limits)
  │
  └──► PostgreSQL (source of truth, durable data)
Cache Store TTL Invalidation User session Redis 30 min Logout / expiry Product catalog Redis 5 min Pub/sub on PG UPDATE Query result Redis 1 min Version key in cache key

Cache-aside flow:

  1. Read Redis → hit → return
  2. Miss → read PostgreSQL → write Redis → return
  3. Write → update PostgreSQL → delete/invalidate Redis key

docs.title: Pattern B — redis_fdw (Foreign Data Wrapper)
docs.text:
Query Redis from SQL — read-heavy, optional writes.

sudo dnf install postgresql18-redis_fdw   # if available in PGDG

CREATE EXTENSION redis_fdw;

CREATE SERVER redis_server
  FOREIGN DATA WRAPPER redis_fdw
  OPTIONS (address '127.0.0.1', port '6379');

CREATE FOREIGN TABLE redis_cache (
  key text,
  value text
) SERVER redis_server
  OPTIONS (database '0');

SELECT * FROM redis_cache WHERE key = 'user:123';

Use when: SQL-centric apps need cache lookups in joins or reporting.

docs.title: Pattern C — LISTEN/NOTIFY for Cache Invalidation
docs.text:
-- PostgreSQL trigger on update
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION notify_cache_invalidate() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
  PERFORM pg_notify('cache_invalidate', TG_TABLE_NAME || ':' || NEW.id::text);
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER orders_notify
  AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON orders
  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION notify_cache_invalidate();

Application subscribes to cache_invalidate and purges Redis keys.

docs.title: Pattern D — Logical Replication to Read Models
docs.text:
Stream changes to another store (analytics DB, search index, cache warmer).

PostgreSQL (primary) ──logical replication──► Subscriber / Debezium ──► Redis / Elasticsearch

docs.title: Pattern E — PgBouncer (Connection Memory, Not Data Cache)
docs.text:
Each PostgreSQL backend uses ~5–10 MB baseline RAM. PgBouncer multiplexes connections:

1000 app connections ──► PgBouncer (50 pool) ──► PostgreSQL (50 backends)

Reduces connection memory overhead, not data caching. See Connection Pooling.

docs.title: Pattern F — Citus / Distributed (Sharding, Not RAM Store)
docs.text: Citus spreads data across nodes — horizontal scale, not in-memory column store.

docs.title: HA / Patroni Memory Considerations
docs.text:
Topic Guidance All nodes same shared_buffers Yes — consistent performance after failover pg_prewarm after failover Warm critical tables on new primary UNLOGGED on standby Not replicated — only on primary Replication lag Large work_mem sorts on primary increase WAL volume indirectly 
# patroni.yml — memory params (PG 18 example, 64 GB RAM)
postgresql:
  parameters:
    shared_buffers: 16GB
    effective_cache_size: 48GB
    work_mem: 64MB
    maintenance_work_mem: 2GB
    huge_pages: try
    io_method: worker

docs.title: Docker / Kubernetes Memory Limits
docs.text:
# Kubernetes — set limits above shared_buffers + work_mem headroom
resources:
  requests:
    memory: "24Gi"
  limits:
    memory: "28Gi"
env:
  - name: POSTGRES_SHARED_BUFFERS
    value: "8GB"

Rule: Container limit ≥ shared_buffers + (max_connections × ~10MB) + OS headroom.

OOM kill in K8s → pod restart — set shared_buffers conservatively in constrained pods.

docs.title: 8 GB RAM (dev/small)
docs.text:
shared_buffers = 2GB
effective_cache_size = 6GB
work_mem = 16MB
maintenance_work_mem = 256MB
max_connections = 50

docs.title: 64 GB RAM (production OLTP)
docs.text:
shared_buffers = 16GB
effective_cache_size = 48GB
work_mem = 64MB
maintenance_work_mem = 2GB
max_connections = 200
huge_pages = try
io_method = worker
shared_preload_libraries = pg_stat_statements,pg_prewarm

docs.title: 256 GB RAM (analytics / mixed)
docs.text:
shared_buffers = 32GB
effective_cache_size = 192GB
work_mem = 256MB
maintenance_work_mem = 4GB
max_parallel_workers = 16

docs.title: Monitoring Checklist
docs.text:
-- Cache hit ratio
SELECT datname, blks_hit, blks_read,
       round(blks_hit::numeric / nullif(blks_hit + blks_read, 0) * 100, 2) AS hit_pct
FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname = current_database();

-- Memory-related waits
SELECT wait_event_type, wait_event, count(*)
FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE wait_event IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1, 2;

-- Temp file usage (work_mem too low)
SELECT datname, temp_files, pg_size_pretty(temp_bytes)
FROM pg_stat_database ORDER BY temp_bytes DESC;

Prometheus / postgres_exporter: track pg_stat_database_blks_hit, pg_stat_database_temp_bytes.

docs.title: What PostgreSQL Does NOT Have (vs Other Databases)
docs.text: Feature Oracle / SQL Server PostgreSQL 18 In-memory column store Yes No — use OS cache + shared_buffers Buffer pool per table Partial shared_buffers shared globally Native Redis integration No No — app layer or redis_fdw All-RAM database mode TimesTen etc. No — use UNLOGGED + risk acceptance

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture — Memory
  • Tuning Parameters
  • postgresql.conf
  • Parameters Quick Reference
  • Connection Pooling
  • pg_cron
  • PG 18 Features

docs.title: Index Types
docs.text: Type Best for Operator class B-tree (default) =, <, >, BETWEEN, LIKE 'prefix%' default Hash Equality only — GIN jsonb, arrays, full-text jsonb_ops, jsonb_path_ops GiST Geometry, ranges, exclusion varies BRIN Large sorted tables (timestamps) minmax, bloom SP-GiST Text patterns, IP networks —

docs.title: Create Indexes
docs.text:
-- Standard
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer ON orders (customer_id);

-- Composite (column order matters)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_cust_date ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC);

-- Partial — index subset of rows
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_open ON orders (created_at)
  WHERE status IN ('pending', 'processing');

-- Expression
CREATE INDEX idx_users_lower_email ON users (lower(email));

-- Covering index (INCLUDE — PG 11+)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_cover ON orders (customer_id) INCLUDE (total, status);

-- Concurrently (no write blocking — use in production)
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_status ON orders (status);

docs.title: When to Index
docs.text:
• WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY columns with high selectivity
  • Foreign key columns (PostgreSQL does not auto-index FKs)
  • Columns used in partial index predicates

docs.title: When NOT to Index
docs.text:
• Small tables (seq scan cheaper)
  • Low selectivity (boolean flags) — use partial index instead
  • Write-heavy tables with unused indexes
-- Find unused indexes
SELECT schemaname, relname, indexrelname, idx_scan, idx_tup_read,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0 AND indexrelname NOT LIKE '%pkey%'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;

docs.title: Duplicate / Redundant Indexes
docs.text:
-- idx (a,b) makes idx (a) redundant in many cases
SELECT indexrelid::regclass, indrelid::regclass, indkey
FROM pg_index JOIN pg_class ON indexrelid = pg_class.oid;

docs.title: Index Maintenance
docs.text:
REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_status;
REINDEX TABLE CONCURRENTLY orders;   -- PG 12+

B-tree bloat: check with pgstatindex.

docs.title: Full-Text Search
docs.text:
ALTER TABLE articles ADD COLUMN search_vector tsvector
  GENERATED ALWAYS AS (to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(body,''))) STORED;

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_fts ON articles USING gin (search_vector);

SELECT * FROM articles WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & tuning');

docs.title: JSONB Indexing
docs.text:
CREATE INDEX idx_data_gin ON events USING gin (payload jsonb_path_ops);
SELECT * FROM events WHERE payload @> '{"type": "click"}';

CREATE INDEX idx_data_path ON events ((payload->>'user_id'));

docs.title: BRIN for Time-Series
docs.text:
CREATE INDEX idx_logs_time ON logs USING brin (logged_at)
  WITH (pages_per_range = 128);

Tiny index size vs. B-tree on billions of rows.

docs.title: Exclusion Constraints
docs.text:
CREATE EXTENSION btree_gist;
CREATE TABLE reservations (
  room_id int,
  during tstzrange,
  EXCLUDE USING gist (room_id WITH =, during WITH &&)
);

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Query Optimization
  • Partitioning

docs.title: pgbench — Load Testing & Benchmarking
docs.text:
pgbench ships with PostgreSQL client tools. Use it to measure TPS, latency, and regression-test tuning changes on PostgreSQL 18.

Binary path (RHEL PGDG): /usr/pgsql-18/bin/pgbench

docs.title: When to Use pgbench
docs.text:
Scenario Approach Baseline after install Default TPC-B-like workload Before/after tuning Same scale factor, clients, duration HA failover impact Run during/after Patroni switchover PG version upgrade Compare PG 17 vs PG 18 on identical hardware Connection pool sizing Sweep -c with PgBouncer in front

Not for: realistic application query mixes — use custom scripts or tools like HammerDB, sysbench, or replay from pg_stat_statements.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Create test database
createdb -h localhost -p 5432 pgbench_test

# Initialize schema (creaters pgbench_accounts, branches, tellers, history)
pgbench -i -s 50 pgbench_test
# -s 50 ≈ 7.5M rows in pgbench_accounts (~1 GB data)

# Run 60-second benchmark: 10 clients, 2 threads
pgbench -c 10 -j 2 -T 60 pgbench_test

Sample output:

transaction type: <builtin: TPC-B (sort of)>
scaling factor: 50
query mode: simple
number of clients: 10
number of threads: 2
duration: 60 s
number of transactions actually processed: 45231
latency average = 13.245 ms
initial connection time = 8.412 ms
tps = 753.821 (without initial connection time)

docs.title: Initialize (-i) Options
docs.text:
pgbench -i \
  -s 100 \              # scale factor (rows in accounts = 100000 × s)
  --fillfactor=90 \     # leave page headroom like production tables
  --unlogged \          # faster init; tables not crash-safe
  --foreign-keys \      # add FK constraints (more realistic, slower)
  pgbench_test
Scale factor ~accounts rows ~DB size 10 1M ~150 MB 50 5M ~750 MB 100 10M ~1.5 GB 1000 100M ~15 GB 
-- Verify init
SELECT relname, n_live_tup, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(oid))
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
JOIN pg_class USING (relname)
WHERE schemaname = 'public' AND relname LIKE 'pgbench%';

docs.title: Duration vs transaction count
docs.text:
pgbench -c 32 -j 8 -T 300 pgbench_test    # 5 minutes
pgbench -c 32 -j 8 -t 10000 pgbench_test  # exactly 10k xacts per client

docs.title: Client / thread tuning
docs.text:
# Rule: threads ≤ CPU cores; clients = expected concurrent sessions
pgbench -c 64 -j 16 -T 120 pgbench_test

docs.title: Connection modes
docs.text:
pgbench --protocol=simple pgbench_test   # default; one query per round trip
pgbench --protocol=extended pgbench_test # parse/bind/execute (PgBouncer friendly)
pgbench --protocol=prepared pgbench_test # prepared statements

Use extended or prepared when benchmarking through PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode.

docs.title: Rate limiting
docs.text:
# Cap at 500 TPS (useful for steady load tests)
pgbench -c 20 -j 4 -T 60 --rate=500 pgbench_test

docs.title: Progress reporting
docs.text: pgbench -c 16 -j 4 -T 300 -P 5 pgbench_test   # report every 5 seconds

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
pgbench -c 10 -j 2 -T 60 -l pgbench_test
# Creates pgbench_log.NNN with per-transaction latency (ms)

Analyze latencies:

sort -n pgbench_log.123 | awk '
  { a[NR]=$1; sum+=$1 }
  END {
    print "count", NR, "avg", sum/NR
    print "p50", a[int(NR*0.50)]
    print "p95", a[int(NR*0.95)]
    print "p99", a[int(NR*0.99)]
  }'

docs.title: Single SQL file
docs.text:
cat > /tmp/select_only.sql <<'EOF'
\set aid random(1, 100000 * :scale)
SELECT abalance FROM pgbench_accounts WHERE aid = :aid;
EOF

pgbench -c 20 -j 4 -T 60 -f /tmp/select_only.sql -n pgbench_test
# -n = no vacuum/analyze between runs

docs.title: Weighted transaction mix
docs.text:
cat > /tmp/read.sql <<'EOF'
\set aid random(1, 100000 * :scale)
SELECT abalance FROM pgbench_accounts WHERE aid = :aid;
EOF

cat > /tmp/write.sql <<'EOF'
\set aid random(1, 100000 * :scale)
\set delta random(-5000, 5000)
BEGIN;
UPDATE pgbench_accounts SET abalance = abalance + :delta WHERE aid = :aid;
SELECT abalance FROM pgbench_accounts WHERE aid = :aid;
END;
EOF

pgbench -c 32 -j 8 -T 120 \
  -f /tmp/read.sql@8 \
  -f /tmp/write.sql@2 \
  pgbench_test
# @8 @2 = 80% reads, 20% writes

docs.title: Built-in variables
docs.text: Variable Meaning :scale Init scale factor :client_id Client number random(min, max) Inclusive random integer random(1, 100000 * :scale) Standard account key range

docs.title: Benchmark Methodology (Production-Safe)
docs.text:
1. Never run heavy pgbench on production without change window — use a clone or staging cluster.
  2. Warm cache: run a short throwaway test before the measured run.
  3. Fix variables: same -s, -c, -j, -T, hardware, postgresql.conf.
  4. Reset stats between A/B tests:
SELECT pg_stat_reset_shared('bgwriter');
SELECT pg_stat_reset();
-- Or restart cluster for clean buffer state
  1. Capture context:
pgbench -c 32 -j 8 -T 300 -P 10 pgbench_test 2>&1 | tee pgbench_$(date +%Y%m%d).log

-- During run
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname = 'pgbench_test';
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_bgwriter;
SELECT * FROM pg_aios;   -- PG 18 AIO activity

docs.title: Before/after shared_buffers
docs.text:
# Baseline
pgbench -c 32 -j 8 -T 180 pgbench_test | tee before.txt

# After ALTER SYSTEM SET shared_buffers = '8GB'; restart
pgbench -c 32 -j 8 -T 180 pgbench_test | tee after.txt

docs.title: PG 18 AIO comparison
docs.text:
# Test 1
io_method = none
# Test 2
io_method = worker

Run identical pgbench after cold restart; compare TPS and pg_stat_io.

docs.title: Connection pool comparison
docs.text:
# Direct to PostgreSQL
pgbench -h pg-primary -c 100 -j 8 -T 120 pgbench_test

# Through PgBouncer (transaction mode)
pgbench -h pgbouncer -p 6432 -c 500 -j 8 -T 120 --protocol=extended pgbench_test

docs.title: HA Failover Test Pattern
docs.text:
# Terminal 1 — continuous load
pgbench -h haproxy-vip -p 5000 -c 20 -j 4 -T 600 -P 5 pgbench_test

# Terminal 2 — trigger failover (Patroni example)
patronictl switchover --master pg-node1 --candidate pg-node2

Watch for latency spikes and connection errors during promotion.

docs.title: Interpreting Results
docs.text:
Metric Good sign Investigate if tps Stable across run Drops after warmup (checkpoint, autovacuum) latency average Low vs SLA Spikes at -P intervals initial connection time < 50 ms High with SSL or many -c failed transactions 0 Any failures — check logs

Cross-check with:

SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time, stddev_exec_time
FROM pg_stat_statements
WHERE query LIKE '%pgbench%'
ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC;

(requires shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements')

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Problem Cause Fix ERROR: relation "pgbench_accounts" does not exist Not initialized pgbench -i TPS flatlines low Disk-bound cold cache Warm run; check pg_stat_io too many clients -c > max_connections Lower clients or raise limit / use PgBouncer Different results each run Autovacuum, checkpoint -n flag; longer -T; disable autovacuum on test DB only SSL overhead -h with sslmode=require Expected; compare with/without for planning

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
dropdb pgbench_test
# Or keep for regression suite

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• In-Memory Features & Integration
  • Tuning Parameters
  • Query Optimization
  • pg_stat_statements
  • PG 18 HA Runbook

docs.title: EXPLAIN Basics
docs.text:
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 123;

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT o.*, c.name
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.created_at > now() - interval '30 days';
Option Purpose ANALYZE Actually runs query — real timings BUFFERS Shared/local block hits & reads VERBOSE Column details WAL WAL records generated (PG 13+) SETTINGS Modified planner settings FORMAT JSON Machine-readable for tools

docs.title: Reading a Plan
docs.text:
Nested Loop  (cost=0.43..16.48 rows=1 width=100) (actual time=0.020..0.025 rows=1 loops=1)
  ->  Index Scan using orders_customer_idx on orders  (cost=0.43..8.45 rows=1 width=80)
        Index Cond: (customer_id = 123)
  ->  Index Scan using customers_pkey on customers  (cost=0.15..8.17 rows=1 width=20)
        Index Cond: (id = 123)
Node Meaning Seq Scan Full table scan Index Scan Index lookup + heap fetch Index Only Scan Satisfied from index + VM Bitmap Index/Heap Scan Multiple index matches combined Nested Loop For each outer row, scan inner Hash Join Build hash on inner, probe outer Merge Join Sorted inputs merged Sort Explicit sort Aggregate GROUP BY / aggregates

Red flags: Seq Scan on large tables, high actual vs. estimated rows, nested loop with huge outer rows.

docs.title: Fix Bad Estimates
docs.text:
ANALYZE orders;

-- Extended statistics
CREATE STATISTICS orders_stats (dependencies, ndistinct)
  ON customer_id, status FROM orders;
ANALYZE orders;

-- Increase stats target
ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN status SET STATISTICS 1000;
ANALYZE orders;

docs.title: Enable/Disable Plan Types (Testing)
docs.text:
SET enable_seqscan = off;   -- force index use (debug only)
SET enable_nestloop = off;
RESET ALL;

docs.title: pg_stat_statements
docs.text:
CREATE EXTENSION pg_stat_statements;

SELECT queryid, calls,
       round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms,
       round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms,
       rows, shared_blks_hit, shared_blks_read
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 20;

Reset stats: SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset();

docs.title: 1. Missing Index
docs.text: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY ON orders (created_at) WHERE status = 'pending';

docs.title: 2. SELECT * Avoidance
docs.text: Fetch only needed columns; enables index-only scans.

docs.title: 3. Pagination
docs.text:
-- Bad: OFFSET 100000
-- Better: keyset pagination
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id > 100000 ORDER BY id LIMIT 50;

docs.title: 4. Function on Column
docs.text:
-- Bad: WHERE lower(email) = 'x@y.com'  (no index)
-- Good: WHERE email = 'X@Y.com' with functional index, or store normalized

docs.title: 5. OR Conditions
docs.text:
-- May need UNION ALL instead of OR for index use
SELECT * FROM t WHERE a = 1
UNION ALL
SELECT * FROM t WHERE b = 2 AND a <> 1;

docs.title: Join Order & Statistics
docs.text:
PostgreSQL chooses join order automatically. For complex queries:

-- PG 12+ explicit join order (CTE materialization changed PG 12)
WITH big AS MATERIALIZED (SELECT ... heavy ...)
SELECT ... FROM big JOIN ...;

docs.title: Prepared Statements
docs.text:
PREPARE get_order(bigint) AS SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = $1;
EXECUTE get_order(123);

Generic vs. custom plans: plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan for skewed params.

docs.title: auto_explain
docs.text:
shared_preload_libraries = 'auto_explain'
auto_explain.log_min_duration = '1s'
auto_explain.log_analyze = true
auto_explain.log_buffers = true

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• explain.depesz.com — visual EXPLAIN
  • pev2 — PostgreSQL Explain Visualizer
  • pgMustard — plan analysis hints

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Indexing
  • Slow Queries
  • pg_stat_statements

docs.title: Session & Timeout Tuning
docs.text:
Prevent runaway queries, idle transactions, and lock pile-ups on PostgreSQL 18 production clusters.

See also: postgresql.conf · Connection Pooling · Locking

docs.title: Key Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Scope Default Purpose statement_timeout Session/role/db 0 (off) Kill queries exceeding duration lock_timeout Session 0 Fail if lock not acquired in time idle_in_transaction_session_timeout Session 0 Kill idle sessions inside open tx idle_session_timeout Session 0 Kill idle sessions (PG 14+, not in tx) transaction_timeout Session 0 Max transaction duration (PG 17+)

All accept ms suffix: '30s', '5000', '1min'.

docs.title: Recommended Production Defaults
docs.text:
# postgresql.conf — global safety net
statement_timeout = 0              # off globally; set per role
lock_timeout = 0
idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = 0
idle_session_timeout = 0

-- Application role
ALTER ROLE app_user SET statement_timeout = '30s';
ALTER ROLE app_user SET lock_timeout = '5s';
ALTER ROLE app_user SET idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '60s';

-- Reporting / batch (higher limits)
ALTER ROLE analyst SET statement_timeout = '30min';
ALTER ROLE analyst SET lock_timeout = '30s';

-- Migration role (change window only)
ALTER ROLE migrator SET statement_timeout = '0';
ALTER ROLE migrator SET lock_timeout = '10s';

docs.title: statement_timeout
docs.text:
Aborts running query after limit.

SET statement_timeout = '5s';
SELECT pg_sleep(10);   -- ERROR: canceling statement due to statement timeout

PgBouncer note: In transaction pooling, use role-level ALTER ROLE ... SET so each server connection inherits settings.

docs.title: lock_timeout
docs.text:
Prevents indefinite wait on locks.

SET lock_timeout = '3s';
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE orders IN ACCESS EXCLUSIVE MODE;  -- fails fast if blocked

Useful for online DDL with fallback:

SET lock_timeout = '2s';
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN foo int;  -- retry if fails

docs.title: idle_in_transaction_session_timeout
docs.text:
Kills sessions that hold a transaction open without activity — critical for OLTP.

ALTER ROLE app_user SET idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '60s';

Common app bug: open transaction after SELECT, connection returned to pool with tx open → blocks vacuum, holds locks.

Monitor:

SELECT pid, usename, state, xact_start, state_change,
       now() - xact_start AS tx_age, left(query, 60)
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'idle in transaction'
ORDER BY xact_start;

docs.title: idle_session_timeout (PG 14+)
docs.text:
Closes completely idle connections (not in transaction). Use for leaked connections.

ALTER ROLE app_user SET idle_session_timeout = '10min';

docs.title: transaction_timeout (PG 17+)
docs.text:
Hard cap on total transaction wall time including locks and idle.

ALTER ROLE app_user SET transaction_timeout = '5min';

docs.title: Session vs Global
docs.text:
-- Session override
SET LOCAL statement_timeout = '120s';  -- transaction-scoped with SET LOCAL

-- Check effective value
SELECT name, setting, unit, source
FROM pg_settings
WHERE name LIKE '%timeout%';

Priority: session > role > database > global.

docs.title: PgBouncer Integration
docs.text:
; pgbouncer.ini
server_idle_timeout = 600
client_idle_timeout = 0
query_timeout = 0        ; prefer PG statement_timeout

Transaction mode resets session state — rely on ALTER ROLE ... SET not session SET in app.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
SELECT pid, usename, state, wait_event,
       now() - query_start AS query_age,
       left(query, 80)
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'active' AND query_start < now() - interval '30 seconds';

Alert on log pattern: canceling statement due to statement timeout spike → tune query or raise role limit.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Cluster Management
  • Investigation Runbook
  • Slow Queries

docs.title: Memory & I/O Tuning Parameters
docs.text: Deep dive: In-Memory Features & Integration — shared_buffers, work_mem, huge pages, pg_prewarm, Redis integration.

docs.title: RAM Sizing Framework
docs.text:
For a dedicated PostgreSQL server:

shared_buffers     = 25% of RAM (cap ~8-16 GB on Linux)
effective_cache_size = 50-75% of RAM
work_mem           = calculated (see below)
maintenance_work_mem = 5-10% of RAM (cap ~2 GB unless bulk ops)

docs.title: work_mem Calculation
docs.text:
work_mem ≈ (RAM - shared_buffers) / (max_connections × 3)

Example: 32 GB RAM, 200 connections: 

(32GB - 8GB) / (200 × 3) ≈ 40 MB

Use lower global default; raise per-session for reporting roles.

docs.title: I/O Settings (SSD vs HDD)
WAL & Checkpoint Tuning
docs.text:
High write throughput:

max_wal_size = 8GB
min_wal_size = 2GB
checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9
wal_compression = on
wal_buffers = 64MB

Spread checkpoint I/O:

checkpoint writes ≈ shared_buffers dirty pages over (checkpoint_timeout × completion_target)

docs.title: Connection & Parallelism
docs.text:
max_connections = 100          # use PgBouncer beyond this
max_worker_processes = 16
max_parallel_workers = 8
max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 4
max_parallel_maintenance_workers = 4

-- Per-query parallel (PG 14+)
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 8;

docs.title: Planner Statistics
docs.text:
default_statistics_target = 100   # 500 for skewed columns
constraint_exclusion = partition  # for partitioned tables

ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN status SET STATISTICS 500;
CREATE STATISTICS orders_corr (dependencies) ON customer_id, status FROM orders;
ANALYZE orders;

docs.title: Vacuum & Bloat Parameters
docs.text:
autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.05   # lower for hot tables
autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.02
vacuum_cost_delay = 0                   # PG 13+ default 0

Per-table:

ALTER TABLE hot_table SET (
  autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.01,
  autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01,
  fillfactor = 90
);

docs.title: JIT Compilation (PG 11+)
docs.text:
jit = on
jit_above_cost = 100000
jit_inline_above_cost = 500000

Disable for OLTP if overhead exceeds benefit:

ALTER DATABASE app_db SET jit = off;

docs.title: Huge Pages (Linux)
docs.text:
# Estimate: shared_buffers in pages × 2MB
grep HugePages /proc/meminfo

# sysctl
vm.nr_hugepages = 4096

huge_pages = try   # or on

docs.title: Monitoring Tuning Impact
docs.text:
SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time, total_exec_time
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 20;

Compare before/after with same workload.

docs.title: PG 18 — Shared Memory Visibility
docs.text:
SHOW shared_memory_size;
SHOW shared_memory_size_in_huge_pages;
SELECT * FROM pg_backend_memory_contexts WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid() ORDER BY total_bytes DESC LIMIT 10;

docs.title: PG 18: Asynchronous I/O
docs.text:
io_method = worker
io_combine_limit = 128kB
io_max_combine_limit = 1MB
effective_io_concurrency = 200    # more useful on PG 18 without fadvise
maintenance_io_concurrency = 200

SELECT * FROM pg_aios;

After upgrading to PG 18, benchmark sequential scans and VACUUM duration before/after enabling AIO — see pgbench.

docs.title: 4 GB RAM (small dev/staging)
docs.text:
shared_buffers = 1GB
effective_cache_size = 3GB
work_mem = 16MB
maintenance_work_mem = 256MB
max_connections = 50

docs.title: 64 GB RAM (production OLTP)
docs.text:
shared_buffers = 16GB
effective_cache_size = 48GB
work_mem = 64MB
maintenance_work_mem = 2GB
max_connections = 200
max_wal_size = 8GB
effective_io_concurrency = 200
random_page_cost = 1.1

docs.title: 256 GB RAM (analytics / mixed)
docs.text:
shared_buffers = 32GB
effective_cache_size = 192GB
work_mem = 256MB
maintenance_work_mem = 4GB
max_parallel_workers = 16

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• postgresql.conf
  • Query Optimization
  • VACUUM & Bloat

docs.title: Why VACUUM Exists
docs.text:
PostgreSQL MVCC leaves dead tuples after UPDATE/DELETE. VACUUM:

  1. Reclaims space for reuse (does not return to OS except edge cases)
  2. Updates visibility map (enables index-only scans)
  3. Freezes old XIDs (prevents wraparound)
  4. Updates planner statistics (with ANALYZE)

docs.title: Manual VACUUM
docs.text:
VACUUM VERBOSE orders;
VACUUM (VERBOSE, ANALYZE) orders;
VACUUM FULL orders;           -- rewrites table, exclusive lock — avoid in prod
VACUUM (PARALLEL 4) big_table;  -- PG 13+ index vacuum parallel

docs.title: Bloat Detection
docs.text:
-- pg_stat_user_tables
SELECT schemaname, relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup,
       round(n_dead_tup * 100.0 / nullif(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) AS dead_pct,
       last_vacuum, last_autovacuum
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC;

Use pgstattuple extension for precise bloat:

CREATE EXTENSION pgstattuple;
SELECT * FROM pgstattuple('orders');
SELECT * FROM pgstatindex('orders_pkey');

docs.title: Reclaim Space to OS
docs.text:
Method Lock Returns disk VACUUM ShareUpdateExclusive Rarely VACUUM FULL AccessExclusive Yes pg_repack Minimal Yes CLUSTER AccessExclusive Partial

pg_repack (extension) — online rewrite:

pg_repack -d app_db -t orders

docs.title: Transaction ID Wraparound
docs.text:
SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) AS age,
       current_setting('autovacuum_freeze_max_age')::int AS max_age
FROM pg_database
ORDER BY age DESC;

Emergency: VACUUM FREEZE VERBOSE; ensure autovacuum not blocked.

docs.title: Fillfactor
docs.text:
ALTER TABLE orders SET (fillfactor = 80);
-- leaves 20% page space for HOT updates (same-page updates without index churn)

docs.title: HOT Updates
docs.text:
Updates avoiding indexed columns may stay on same page (Heap-Only Tuple) — no new index entries.

Monitor:

SELECT relname, n_tup_upd, n_tup_hot_upd,
       round(n_tup_hot_upd * 100.0 / nullif(n_tup_upd, 0), 1) AS hot_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables;

docs.title: Toast Bloat
docs.text:
Oversized values stored in TOAST tables:

SELECT relname, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid))
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC;

docs.title: Long Transactions Block VACUUM
docs.text:
SELECT pid, usename, state, xact_start, query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE xact_start IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY xact_start;

Idle in transaction = vacuum blocker.

docs.title: Prepared Transactions
docs.text:
SELECT * FROM pg_prepared_xacts;  -- blocks vacuum on involved rows
COMMIT PREPARED 'gid';

docs.title: Table & Index Maintenance Schedule
docs.text: Frequency Action Continuous autovacuum Weekly Review dead_pct, bloat queries Monthly pg_repack on bloated tables Quarterly REINDEX CONCURRENTLY on degraded indexes

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Autovacuum
  • Indexing
  • Architecture — MVCC

docs.title: DBA Health Checks & Monitoring Queries
docs.text:
Copy-paste SQL and shell checks for daily automation or on-call triage on PostgreSQL 18.

Alerts wiring: Metrics Exporters · Checklists: DBA Runbook

docs.title: Quick Status (30 seconds)
docs.text:
SELECT version();
SELECT pg_is_in_recovery() AS standby,
       pg_postmaster_start_time(),
       current_setting('cluster_name') AS cluster;
SELECT count(*) AS connections,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE state = 'active') AS active,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE wait_event IS NOT NULL) AS waiting
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE pid <> pg_backend_pid();

pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432
df -h /data/pgdata /data/pgarchive /data/pglog 2>/dev/null
sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=main check 2>/dev/null || true

docs.title: 1. Availability & Recovery
docs.text:
-- Standby lag (on primary)
SELECT application_name, client_addr, state, sync_state,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(sent_lsn, replay_lsn) AS replay_lag_bytes,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(sent_lsn, replay_lsn)) AS replay_lag
FROM pg_stat_replication;

-- Standby perspective
SELECT pg_last_wal_receive_lsn(), pg_last_wal_replay_lsn(),
       pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp(),
       now() - pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS replay_delay;

Alert if: replay delay > SLA (e.g. 60s) or lag bytes growing unbounded.

docs.title: 2. WAL Archive (PITR readiness)
docs.text:
SELECT archived_count, failed_count,
       last_archived_wal, last_archived_time,
       last_failed_wal, last_failed_time
FROM pg_stat_archiver;

Alert if: failed_count > 0 or last_archived_time stale > 15 min on busy system.

pgbackrest --stanza=main info | grep -E 'full backup|status'

docs.title: 3. Replication Slots
docs.text:
SELECT slot_name, slot_type, active, temporary,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) AS retained_wal_bytes,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS retained
FROM pg_replication_slots
ORDER BY retained_wal_bytes DESC NULLS LAST;

Alert if: inactive slot with retained WAL > 1 GB (disk fill risk).

See Replication Slots.

docs.title: 4. Disk & Database Size
docs.text:
SELECT datname, pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) AS size
FROM pg_database ORDER BY pg_database_size(datname) DESC;

SELECT relname, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS heap
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC LIMIT 10;

df -h | awk '$5+0 > 80 {print}'   # filesystems > 80%
du -sh $PGDATA/pg_wal

docs.title: 5. Connections & Locks
docs.text:
SELECT datname, usename, state, wait_event_type, wait_event,
       now() - state_change AS state_age,
       left(query, 100) AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE pid <> pg_backend_pid()
ORDER BY state_change;

-- Blockers
SELECT blocked.pid AS blocked_pid,
       blocking.pid AS blocking_pid,
       left(blocked.query, 60) AS blocked_query
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON blocking.pid = ANY(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid))
WHERE cardinality(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid)) > 0;

-- Lock count
SELECT mode, count(*) FROM pg_locks GROUP BY mode ORDER BY count DESC;

Alert if: connections > 80% max_connections; long idle in transaction > 5 min.

docs.title: 6. Vacuum & Bloat
docs.text:
SELECT schemaname, relname, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup,
       last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze,
       round(n_dead_tup * 100.0 / nullif(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 1) AS dead_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC LIMIT 15;

-- XID wraparound risk
SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) AS xid_age,
       current_setting('autovacuum_freeze_max_age')::bigint AS max_age
FROM pg_database ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC;

Alert if: xid_age > 200M (investigate); > 1.5B critical.

docs.title: 7. Performance — Cache & I/O
docs.text:
SELECT datname,
       round(blks_hit * 100.0 / nullif(blks_hit + blks_read, 0), 2) AS cache_hit_pct
FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname NOT LIKE 'template%';

SELECT checkpoints_timed, checkpoints_req, buffers_checkpoint,
       buffers_clean, maxwritten_clean, checkpoint_write_time
FROM pg_stat_bgwriter;

Alert if: cache hit < 95% on OLTP (after warmup); checkpoints_req dominating.

-- PG 18 AIO
SELECT * FROM pg_aios;
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_io LIMIT 20;

docs.title: 8. Slow Queries (pg_stat_statements)
docs.text:
SELECT calls, round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms,
       round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms,
       rows, left(query, 120) AS query
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 15;

SELECT calls, temp_blks_written, left(query, 100)
FROM pg_stat_statements
WHERE temp_blks_written > 0
ORDER BY temp_blks_written DESC LIMIT 10;

docs.title: 9. Invalid & Unused Indexes
docs.text:
SELECT indexrelid::regclass AS index, indisvalid, indisready
FROM pg_index WHERE NOT indisvalid;

SELECT schemaname, relname, indexrelname, idx_scan,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0 AND indexrelname NOT LIKE 'pg_toast%'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC LIMIT 15;

docs.title: 10. Security Spot Checks
docs.text:
SELECT rolname, rolsuper, rolcreatedb, rolreplication, rolcreaterole
FROM pg_roles WHERE rolsuper AND rolname NOT LIKE 'pg_%';

SHOW password_encryption;
SELECT rolname, rolpassword LIKE 'md5%' AS is_md5 FROM pg_authid WHERE rolcanlogin;

docs.title: Health Report Script
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# /usr/local/bin/pg-health.sh
OUT=/var/log/pg-health-$(date +%Y%m%d).log
{
  echo "=== $(date) ==="
  pg_isready
  psql -X -c "SELECT version();"
  psql -X -c "SELECT * FROM pg_stat_archiver;"
  psql -X -c "SELECT * FROM pg_stat_replication;"
  psql -X -c "SELECT slot_name, active, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) FROM pg_replication_slots;"
  psql -X -c "SELECT count(*), state FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state;"
  df -h /data/pgdata /data/pgarchive 2>/dev/null
  pgbackrest --stanza=main info 2>/dev/null | head -20
} >> "$OUT" 2>&1

Cron: 0 */6 * * * postgres /usr/local/bin/pg-health.sh

docs.title: Alert Threshold Summary
docs.text: Metric Warning Critical Disk usage 80% 90% Replication lag > 30s > 5 min Archive failures any in 1h sustained Connections 80% max 95% max Cache hit (OLTP) < 97% < 90% xid_age > 200M > 1B Slot retained WAL > 500 MB > 5 GB Last full backup > 8 days > 14 days

docs.title: Logging Configuration
docs.text: Dedicated log path setup: Log Directory

docs.title: Core Settings
docs.text:
logging_collector = on
log_directory = 'log'
log_filename = 'postgresql-%Y-%m-%d_%H%M%S.log'
log_rotation_age = 1d
log_rotation_size = 100MB
log_truncate_on_rotation = off

log_line_prefix = '%m [%p] %u@%d %a %h %x '
# %m timestamp  %p pid  %u user  %d db  %a app  %h host  %x xid

log_timezone = 'UTC'
log_min_messages = warning
log_min_error_statement = error

docs.title: Query Logging
docs.text:
# Log queries exceeding duration (ms)
log_min_duration_statement = 500

# Log all statements (dev only — high volume)
# log_statement = 'all'    # none | ddl | mod | all

log_duration = off
log_statement_sample_rate = 1.0   # PG 14+ fraction of statements

Per-role override:

ALTER ROLE analyst SET log_min_duration_statement = 100;

docs.title: Connections & Auth
docs.text:
log_connections = on
log_disconnections = on
log_hostname = on
log_password_error = on   # PG 14+ — does not log password

docs.title: Locks & Deadlocks
docs.text:
log_lock_waits = on
deadlock_timeout = 1s     # time before checking deadlock

Deadlocks appear automatically in log.

docs.title: Checkpoints & Autovacuum
docs.text:
log_checkpoints = on
log_autovacuum_min_duration = 0   # log all autovacuum runs

docs.title: CSV Log for pgBadger
docs.text:
log_destination = 'csvlog'
logging_collector = on

pgbadger /var/lib/pgsql/18/data/log/postgresql-*.csv -o report.html

docs.title: JSON Logging (PG 15+)
docs.text:
log_destination = 'jsonlog'

Structured logs for ELK, Loki, CloudWatch.

docs.title: auto_explain
docs.text:
Log execution plans for slow queries:

shared_preload_libraries = 'auto_explain'
auto_explain.log_min_duration = '1s'
auto_explain.log_analyze = true
auto_explain.log_buffers = true
auto_explain.log_nested_statements = true

docs.title: Log Analysis Queries (from csvlog table — if loaded)
docs.text: Or use external tools: pgBadger, pganalyze, Grafana Loki.

docs.title: What to Alert On
docs.text: Log pattern Severity PANIC / FATAL Critical ERROR: duplicate key App bug or retry storm deadlock detected Review transaction order canceling statement due to statement timeout Tune query or timeout out of memory Memory tuning could not write to file Disk full archive command failed Backup broken

docs.title: Log Rotation (logrotate)
docs.text:
/var/lib/pgsql/18/data/log/*.log {
    daily
    rotate 14
    compress
    missingok
    notifempty
    copytruncate
    postrotate
        su postgres -c "pg_ctl -D /var/lib/pgsql/18/data reload"
    endscript
}

docs.title: postgres_exporter (Most Common)
docs.text:
# Download from prometheuscommunity/postgres_exporter
export DATA_SOURCE_NAME="postgresql://monitor:pass@localhost:5432/postgres?sslmode=disable"
./postgres_exporter --web.listen-address=:9187

docs.title: Recommended Custom Queries
docs.text:
Place in queries.yaml:

pg_replication:
  query: |
    SELECT client_addr, state,
           pg_wal_lsn_diff(sent_lsn, replay_lsn) AS replay_lag_bytes
    FROM pg_stat_replication
  metrics:
    - client_addr: { usage: LABEL }
      state: { usage: LABEL }
      replay_lag_bytes: { usage: GAUGE }

pg_stat_user_tables:
  query: |
    SELECT schemaname, relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, seq_scan, idx_scan
    FROM pg_stat_user_tables
  metrics:
    - schemaname: { usage: LABEL }
      relname: { usage: LABEL }
      n_live_tup: { usage: GAUGE }
      n_dead_tup: { usage: GAUGE }
      seq_scan: { usage: COUNTER }
      idx_scan: { usage: COUNTER }

docs.title: Monitor User
docs.text:
CREATE USER monitor PASSWORD '...';
GRANT pg_monitor TO monitor;
-- PG 10+: pg_monitor role includes most stats access

docs.title: Key Metrics to Dashboard
docs.text: Metric Source Alert threshold Replication lag (bytes/time) pg_stat_replication > 100MB or > 30s Connections pg_stat_activity count > 80% max_connections Cache hit ratio pg_stat_database < 95% Dead tuples pg_stat_user_tables rising unchecked Long running queries pg_stat_activity > statement_timeout Disk usage node_exporter + du > 85% XID age pg_database > 500M Archive failures pg_stat_archiver failed_count > 0 Lock waits pg_locks blocked > 60s

docs.title: Grafana Dashboards
docs.text:
Import community dashboards: - Dashboard ID 9628 — PostgreSQL Database - Dashboard ID 12485 — PostgreSQL Exporter Quickstart

Use generate_deeplink when sharing with team (if Grafana MCP available).

docs.text:
Alternative all-in-one monitoring (InfluxDB/Prometheus + Grafana):

docker compose up pgwatch2

Auto-discovers metrics, preset dashboards.

docs.text: SaaS — query performance, EXPLAIN plans, index recommendations. Agent on DB host.

docs.title: Cloud Native
docs.text: Platform Monitoring AWS RDS/Aurora CloudWatch, Performance Insights GCP Cloud SQL Cloud Monitoring, Query Insights Azure Flexible Azure Monitor, Query Store Crunchy Bridge Built-in Grafana

docs.text:
- alert: PostgresReplicationLag
  expr: pg_replication_replay_lag_bytes > 1e8
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "PG replication lag > 100MB"

docs.title: pg_stat_statements
docs.text: Tracks normalized SQL execution statistics cluster-wide. Essential for performance monitoring.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# postgresql.conf — requires RESTART
shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'
pg_stat_statements.max = 10000
pg_stat_statements.track = all       # top | all | none
pg_stat_statements.track_utility = on
pg_stat_statements.save = on

CREATE EXTENSION pg_stat_statements;

docs.title: Top by Total Time
docs.text:
SELECT queryid,
       left(query, 100) AS query,
       calls,
       round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms,
       round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms,
       rows,
       shared_blks_hit,
       shared_blks_read,
       temp_blks_written
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 20;

docs.title: Top by Mean Time (Slow Individual Runs)
docs.text:
SELECT left(query, 100), calls,
       round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms,
       round(max_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS max_ms
FROM pg_stat_statements
WHERE calls > 10
ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 20;

docs.title: Most Called
docs.text:
SELECT left(query, 80), calls, rows / calls AS avg_rows
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY calls DESC LIMIT 20;

docs.title: I/O Heavy
docs.text:
SELECT left(query, 80),
       shared_blks_read + shared_blks_dirtied AS io_blocks,
       calls
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY shared_blks_read DESC LIMIT 20;

docs.title: PG 15+ Columns
docs.text: Uses total_exec_time, mean_exec_time (was total_time, mean_time in older versions).

docs.title: Reset Stats
docs.text:
SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset();                    -- all
SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset(12345);               -- by queryid
SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset(0, 0, 123456789::oid); -- by db oid

docs.title: Capture Query Text
docs.text:
SELECT query FROM pg_stat_statements WHERE queryid = 123456789;

With track = all, nested statements inside functions are tracked separately.

docs.title: Integration with Monitoring
docs.text: Export via: - postgres_exporter (Prometheus) — pg_stat_statements custom query - pganalyze - Datadog / New Relic PostgreSQL integrations

docs.title: Security Note
docs.text:
Query text may contain literals (unless using prepared statements). Restrict access:

REVOKE ALL ON pg_stat_statements FROM PUBLIC;
GRANT SELECT ON pg_stat_statements TO pg_monitor;

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Query Optimization
  • Logging
  • Metrics Exporters

docs.title: Essential pg_stat_* Views
docs.text: View Purpose pg_stat_activity Current sessions, queries, wait events pg_stat_replication Replication status (primary) pg_stat_user_tables Seq/index scans, tuples, vacuum stats pg_stat_user_indexes Index usage pg_stat_database DB-level commits, deadlocks, cache hit pg_stat_bgwriter Checkpoint, buffer writes pg_stat_archiver WAL archive success/fail pg_stat_subscription Logical replication (subscriber) pg_stat_io I/O statistics (PG 16+)

docs.title: pg_stat_activity
docs.text:
SELECT pid, usename, datname, state, wait_event_type, wait_event,
       query_start, state_change,
       left(query, 80) AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state != 'idle'
ORDER BY query_start;
state Meaning active Executing query idle Waiting for client idle in transaction Open transaction, no active query idle in transaction (aborted) Failed tx not rolled back

docs.title: Cache Hit Ratio
docs.text:
SELECT datname,
       blks_hit * 100.0 / nullif(blks_hit + blks_read, 0) AS cache_hit_pct
FROM pg_stat_database
WHERE datname = current_database();

Target: > 99% for OLTP; lower may be OK for analytics.

docs.title: Table Access Patterns
docs.text:
SELECT relname, seq_scan, seq_tup_read, idx_scan, idx_tup_fetch,
       n_tup_ins, n_tup_upd, n_tup_del
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY seq_scan DESC;

High seq_scan on large tables → investigate indexes.

docs.title: Lock Inspection
docs.text:
SELECT blocked.pid AS blocked_pid,
       blocked.query AS blocked_query,
       blocking.pid AS blocking_pid,
       blocking.query AS blocking_query
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN pg_locks bl ON bl.pid = blocked.pid AND NOT bl.granted
JOIN pg_locks blkr ON bl.locktype = blkr.locktype
  AND bl.database IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.database
  AND bl.relation IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.relation
  AND bl.page IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.page
  AND bl.tuple IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.tuple
  AND bl.virtualxid IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.virtualxid
  AND bl.transactionid IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.transactionid
  AND bl.classid IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.classid
  AND bl.objid IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.objid
  AND bl.objsubid IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blkr.objsubid
  AND bl.pid != blkr.pid
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON blocking.pid = blkr.pid
WHERE bl.granted = false;

docs.title: Catalog Queries
docs.text:
-- Table definitions
SELECT column_name, data_type, is_nullable, column_default
FROM information_schema.columns
WHERE table_name = 'orders';

-- Index definitions
SELECT indexdef FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'orders';

-- Foreign keys
SELECT conname, conrelid::regclass, confrelid::regclass
FROM pg_constraint WHERE contype = 'f';

-- Table sizes
SELECT relname, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(oid))
FROM pg_class WHERE relkind = 'r' ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(oid) DESC;

docs.title: pg_catalog vs. information_schema
docs.text: Prefer pg_catalog for PG-specific metadata; information_schema is SQL-standard portable.

docs.title: Reset Statistics
docs.text:
SELECT pg_stat_reset();                    -- database stats
SELECT pg_stat_reset_shared('archiver');  -- specific subsystem

docs.title: pgAudit Extension
docs.text:
Full guide: pgAudit (dedicated doc)

Industry standard for detailed audit logging.

shared_preload_libraries = 'pgaudit'
pgaudit.log = 'write, ddl, role'
pgaudit.log_catalog = off
pgaudit.log_parameter = on
pgaudit.log_relation = on
pgaudit.log_statement_once = on

CREATE EXTENSION pgaudit;

-- Role-specific audit
ALTER ROLE app_user SET pgaudit.log = 'write';

Logs appear in PostgreSQL log — ship to SIEM (Splunk, ELK, CloudWatch).

docs.title: Native Logging Audit Trail
docs.text:
log_statement = 'ddl'        # or 'mod' for DML
log_connections = on
log_disconnections = on
log_line_prefix = '%t [%p]: %u@%d '

docs.title: Trigger-Based Audit
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE audit_log (
  id bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
  table_name text,
  operation text,
  old_data jsonb,
  new_data jsonb,
  changed_by text DEFAULT current_user,
  changed_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_trigger_fn() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO audit_log (table_name, operation, old_data, new_data)
  VALUES (TG_TABLE_NAME, TG_OP,
          CASE WHEN TG_OP IN ('UPDATE','DELETE') THEN to_jsonb(OLD) END,
          CASE WHEN TG_OP IN ('INSERT','UPDATE') THEN to_jsonb(NEW) END);
  RETURN COALESCE(NEW, OLD);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER orders_audit
  AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON orders
  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger_fn();

docs.title: Logical Decoding for Change Capture
docs.text:
Stream changes to external systems (Debezium, Kafka):

SELECT pg_create_logical_replication_slot('audit_slot', 'pgoutput');
-- Use Debezium PostgreSQL connector

docs.title: Compliance Checklist
docs.text: Requirement Implementation Who accessed what pgAudit + connection logging Data changes Triggers or logical decoding Retention Log archive policy (1–7 years per regulation) Encryption TLS + disk encryption Least privilege Role separation, RLS Backup audit pgBackRest logs, backup test records

docs.title: PCI / HIPAA / SOC2 Notes
docs.text:
• Disable trust authentication
  • Encrypt data in transit and at rest
  • Regular access reviews (\du, pg_policies)
  • Monitor superuser usage
  • Document break-glass procedures

docs.title: Query Audit Tables
docs.text:
SELECT changed_by, operation, count(*)
FROM audit_log
WHERE changed_at > now() - interval '24 hours'
GROUP BY changed_by, operation;

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Logging
  • Row-Level Security
  • Authentication

docs.title: Password Authentication (SCRAM-SHA-256)
docs.text:
Default in PG 18 (and PG 14+). Secure challenge-response; password never sent in clear. MD5 is deprecated in PG 18 — migrate all roles to SCRAM.

SET password_encryption = 'scram-sha-256';
CREATE ROLE app_user LOGIN PASSWORD 'secure_password';

docs.title: OAuth 2.0 Authentication (PG 18+)
docs.text:
Native SSO integration via OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens — useful for enterprise identity providers.

# postgresql.conf
oauth_validator_libraries = 'oauth_validator'

# pg_hba.conf (method name and options per your validator library)
hostssl all all 0.0.0.0/0 oauth

Requires a loaded OAuth validator shared library and TLS. See PostgreSQL 18 docs — OAuth and postgresql-18.md.

docs.title: MD5 Deprecation (PG 18+)
docs.text:
MD5 password authentication is deprecated and will be removed in a future major release.

-- Migrate roles to SCRAM
ALTER ROLE app_user PASSWORD 'new_password';  -- with password_encryption = scram-sha-256

-- Temporary: suppress deprecation warnings during migration
SET md5_password_warnings = off;

docs.title: Certificate Authentication
docs.text:
# postgresql.conf
ssl = on
ssl_cert_file = 'server.crt'
ssl_key_file = 'server.key'
ssl_ca_file = 'root.crt'

# pg_hba.conf
hostssl all all 0.0.0.0/0 cert clientcert=verify-full

Generate certs with easy-rsa or cfssl. Client cert CN must map to PG role.

docs.title: LDAP / Active Directory
docs.text:
# pg_hba.conf
hostssl all all 0.0.0.0/0 ldap ldapserver=ldap.example.com ldapport=389
  ldapbasedn="dc=example,dc=com" ldapbinddn="cn=pg,dc=example,dc=com"
  ldapbindpasswd=secret ldapsearchattribute=uid

docs.title: GSSAPI / Kerberos
docs.text:
# pg_hba.conf
hostgssenc all all 0.0.0.0/0 gss include_realm=0

Requires Kerberos keytab for postgres service principal.

docs.title: Peer (Local Socket)
docs.text:
local all postgres peer
local all all peer map=appmap

OS username must match or map via pg_ident.conf.

docs.title: OAuth / External IdP
docs.text: Not native — use: - PgBouncer with auth_query + external auth - Cloud IAM (RDS IAM auth, Cloud SQL IAM) - pg_hba + custom auth extensions

docs.title: AWS RDS IAM Authentication
docs.text:
export PGPASSWORD=$(aws rds generate-db-auth-token \
  --hostname mydb.xxx.rds.amazonaws.com --port 5432 --username iam_user)
psql "host=mydb.xxx.rds.amazonaws.com sslmode=require user=iam_user dbname=postgres"

docs.title: Security Hardening Checklist
docs.text:
• SCRAM-SHA-256 for all password roles (MD5 deprecated in PG 18)
  • TLS 1.2+ for all remote connections
  • Revoke PUBLIC privileges on public schema (PG 15 default)
  • Separate roles: app, migration, admin, replication, monitoring
  • No superuser for applications
  • Rotate credentials; use secrets manager
  • Audit failed logins (log_connections)

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• pg_hba.conf
  • Encryption Overview
  • SSL/TLS Implementation
  • TDE at Rest
  • Row-Level Security

docs.title: PostgreSQL Encryption Methods — Complete Reference
docs.text:
All encryption options for PostgreSQL 18 — what each method protects, how it works, and when to use it.

Deep-dive guides TLS setup (certs, mTLS, HA) ssl-tls-implementation.md Data at rest (LUKS, TDE, backups) tde-implementation.md

docs.title: Two Fundamental Layers
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
  IN TRANSIT        │  TLS / SSL (network wire)           │  ← Method 1–4
                    └─────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                         PostgreSQL
                              │
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
  AT REST           │  Volume / TDE / column / backup     │  ← Method 5–12
                    └─────────────────────────────────────┘
Layer Threat model Without encryption In transit Sniffing network, MITM Passwords + query data visible At rest Stolen disk, backup leak, snapshot theft Full database readable

Production baseline: TLS in transit + volume encryption at rest + encrypted backups.

docs.title: Method Comparison (All Options)
docs.text:
# Method Layer Built into PG 18? Transparent? Typical use 1 TLS (server cert) Transit Yes Yes (client config) All remote connections 2 mTLS (client cert) Transit + auth Yes Partial Zero-trust, no passwords 3 TLS at proxy (HAProxy/PgBouncer) Transit Via proxy Yes Centralized cert management 4 Cloud managed TLS (RDS, etc.) Transit Provider Yes Managed PostgreSQL 5 LUKS / dm-crypt At rest OS-level Yes Self-hosted $PGDATA 6 Cloud volume encryption (EBS, Azure, GCP) At rest Cloud Yes VM / managed DB storage 7 pgBackRest backup cipher At rest (backup) Extension/tool Configured Off-site backup protection 8 S3 / object storage SSE-KMS At rest (archive) S3/GCS Yes WAL archive, backup objects 9 pgcrypto (column) At rest (column) Extension No PII columns, tokens 10 Application AES At rest (column) App code No Highest sensitivity fields 11 Percona pg_tde At rest (tablespace) Extension (Percona) Yes True DB-level TDE 12 Password hash (SCRAM) Auth storage Yes Yes Not wire encryption — hashes only

SCRAM-SHA-256 encrypts/hashes stored passwords — it is not a substitute for TLS on the network.

docs.title: Method 1: TLS with Server Certificate (Standard)
docs.text:
What it does: Encrypts all traffic between client and PostgreSQL using TLS 1.2/1.3.

Protects: Query results, passwords (with SCRAM), replication stream on the wire.

# postgresql.conf
ssl = on
ssl_cert_file = '/etc/postgresql/ssl/server/server.crt'
ssl_key_file = '/etc/postgresql/ssl/server/server.key'
ssl_ca_file = '/etc/postgresql/ssl/ca/ca.crt'
ssl_min_protocol_version = 'TLSv1.2'

# pg_hba.conf
hostnossl all all 0.0.0.0/0 reject
hostssl  all all 10.0.2.0/24 scram-sha-256

psql "host=db.example.com sslmode=verify-full sslrootcert=ca.crt dbname=app user=app"
sslmode Use require Encrypted, no cert verify (dev/internal) verify-ca Encrypted + trust CA verify-full Production — encrypted + CA + hostname match

→ ssl-tls-implementation.md

docs.title: Method 2: Mutual TLS (mTLS)
docs.text:
What it does: Server validates client certificate in addition to (or instead of) password.

Protects: Stolen passwords useless without client cert; strong identity.

# pg_hba.conf
hostssl all app_user 10.0.2.0/24 cert clientcert=verify-full

psql "sslmode=verify-full sslrootcert=ca.crt sslcert=client.crt sslkey=client.key ..."

When to use: Service-to-service, zero-trust networks, compliance requiring dual-factor at transport layer.

docs.title: Method 3: TLS Termination at Proxy
docs.text:
What it does: Client connects with TLS to HAProxy/PgBouncer; proxy may use plain or TLS to PostgreSQL backend.

App ──TLS──► HAProxy :5432 ──► PostgreSQL :5432

When to use: Single cert management point; many app clients; internal VPC trusted between proxy and DB.

Risk: Plain text between proxy and DB if not also TLS — acceptable only in private network.

docs.title: Method 4: Cloud Provider TLS
docs.text:
What it does: Amazon RDS, Cloud SQL, Azure issue/manage server certificates.

# RDS — download global CA bundle
psql "host=xxx.rds.amazonaws.com sslmode=verify-full sslrootcert=global-bundle.pem ..."

# RDS parameter
rds.force_ssl = 1

When to use: All managed PostgreSQL — enable storage encrypted + rds.force_ssl at creation.

docs.title: Replication & TLS
docs.text:
Streaming replication supports TLS via hostssl in pg_hba and sslmode in primary_conninfo:

primary_conninfo = 'host=10.0.1.11 port=5432 user=replicator sslmode=verify-full sslrootcert=/etc/postgresql/ssl/ca/ca.crt'

Patroni HA: configure on all nodes — see ssl-tls-implementation.md.

docs.title: Method 5: LUKS Volume Encryption (Linux)
docs.text:
What it does: Encrypts entire block device; PostgreSQL sees normal filesystem.

Algorithm: AES-XTS (LUKS2 default, typically 512-bit key).

cryptsetup luksFormat /dev/nvme1n1
cryptsetup luksOpen /dev/nvme1n1 pgdata_crypt
mkfs.xfs /dev/mapper/pgdata_crypt
mount /dev/mapper/pgdata_crypt /data
# initdb -D /data/pgdata
Pros Cons Transparent to PostgreSQL Key unlock at boot (DR planning) Protects WAL + data + indexes Per-node keys on HA cluster Industry standard CPU overhead minimal on modern HW

→ tde-implementation.md

docs.title: Method 6: Cloud Volume / Managed Storage Encryption
docs.text:
What it does: Hypervisor/storage layer encryption (AES-256).

Platform Feature Key management AWS EBS / RDS --encrypted, --kms-key-id AWS KMS GCP Cloud SQL CMEK Cloud KMS Azure Flexible Infrastructure encryption Azure Key Vault

When to use: Default for all cloud deployments — enable at resource creation (RDS cannot add encryption later without snapshot migration).

docs.title: Method 7: pgBackRest Backup Encryption
docs.text:
What it does: Encrypts backup files in repository (AES-256-CBC).

repo1-cipher-type=aes-256-cbc
repo1-cipher-pass=<passphrase-from-vault>

Protects: Stolen backup files on S3/NFS — independent of $PGDATA volume encryption.

When to use: Always, for off-site backups.

docs.title: Method 8: WAL Archive / Object Storage Encryption
docs.text:
What it does: SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or SSE-C on archived WAL and backup objects.

archive_command = 'aws s3 cp %p s3://pg-wal/%f --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id alias/pg-wal'

When to use: Any WAL shipping to S3/GCS/Azure Blob.

docs.title: Method 9: pgcrypto Column Encryption
docs.text:
What it does: Encrypt specific column values in SQL/application.

CREATE EXTENSION pgcrypto;

INSERT INTO users (ssn_enc)
VALUES (pgp_sym_encrypt('123-45-6789', vault_key));

SELECT pgp_sym_decrypt(ssn_enc, vault_key) FROM users;
Function Algorithm Use pgp_sym_encrypt AES (OpenPGP) Symmetric — single key pgp_pub_encrypt OpenPGP public key Asymmetric digest() SHA-256, etc. Hashing (not encryption) crypt() bcrypt Password hashing

When to use: Specific PII columns; defense in depth beyond disk encryption.

Limitation: Cannot index encrypted values (unless deterministic encryption with tradeoffs).

docs.title: Method 10: Application-Level Encryption
docs.text:
What it does: App encrypts before INSERT; DB stores bytea ciphertext.

# App uses AES-256-GCM + AWS KMS data key
ciphertext = encrypt(plaintext, kms_data_key)
# INSERT into db as bytea

When to use: PAN, SSN, health records; keys never touch PostgreSQL config.

docs.title: Method 11: Percona pg_tde (Extension)
docs.text:
What it does: Transparent encryption of tablespace data files; keys from Vault/KMS.

CREATE EXTENSION pg_tde;
CREATE TABLESPACE enc_ts LOCATION '/data/enc' WITH (encryption = true);
CREATE TABLE secrets (...) TABLESPACE enc_ts;

When to use: Need Oracle/SQL Server-style TDE on PostgreSQL; Percona stack approved.

Not available in community PostgreSQL from postgresql.org.

docs.title: Method 12: SCRAM Password Storage (Auth — Not Wire Encryption)
docs.text:
What it does: Stores password verifier as SCRAM-SHA-256 hash — not reversible.

SHOW password_encryption;  -- scram-sha-256 (PG 18 default)
ALTER ROLE app_user PASSWORD 'secret';

Must pair with TLS — SCRAM protects stored hashes; TLS protects credentials on the wire.

docs.title: Decision Matrix — Which Methods Do I Need?
docs.text: Scenario Recommended methods Standard production (self-hosted) 1 (TLS verify-full) + 5 (LUKS) + 7 (pgBackRest) + 12 (SCRAM) AWS RDS production 4 (force SSL) + 6 (RDS encrypted) + 8 (S3 SSE-KMS backups) Patroni HA 3-node 1 + 5 on each node + TLS replication + 7 PCI / HIPAA 1 + 2 (mTLS optional) + 6 + 7 + 9 or 10 for PAN/PHI + pgAudit Dev / lab 1 (self-signed TLS) or VPN-only + SCRAM Multi-tenant SaaS 1 + 6 + 9/10 for tenant secrets + RLS

docs.title: Encryption Algorithms Reference
docs.text:
Context Algorithm Key size TLS 1.3 AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 256-bit TLS 1.2 (recommended ciphers) ECDHE + AES-GCM 256-bit LUKS2 aes-xts-plain64 512-bit (256-bit effective) pgBackRest AES-256-CBC 256-bit AWS EBS/RDS AES-256 256-bit (KMS-managed) SCRAM-SHA-256 PBKDF2 + SHA-256 — pgcrypto (symmetric) AES (OpenPGP) 128–256 bit pg_tde (Percona) AES KMS-dependent

Avoid: TLS 1.0/1.1, MD5 auth, DES, RC4, sslmode=disable in production.

docs.title: What Is NOT Encrypted (Know the Gaps)
docs.text: Item Encrypted by TLS? Encrypted by LUKS? Notes Query text in logs No N/A Redact logs; restrict log access pg_dump plain output No N/A Use -Fc + encrypt dump file Memory (shared_buffers) No No OS swap encryption optional Replication if plain TCP No N/A Use hostssl + sslmode pg_stat_activity.query No N/A Visible to admins Crash dumps / core files No Partial Disable core dumps on DB servers

docs.title: Step 1: TLS (5 minutes after certs exist)
docs.text:
ssl = on
ssl_cert_file = 'server.crt'
ssl_key_file = 'server.key'
ssl_min_protocol_version = 'TLSv1.2'

hostnossl all all 0.0.0.0/0 reject
hostssl all all 10.0.0.0/8 scram-sha-256

docs.title: Step 2: Encrypted volume
docs.text:
# Cloud: enable encryption at volume/DB creation
# Linux: LUKS on /data before initdb

docs.title: Step 3: Encrypted backups
docs.text:
repo1-cipher-type=aes-256-cbc
repo1-cipher-pass=<vault>

docs.title: Step 4: Verify
docs.text:
SELECT ssl, version, cipher FROM pg_stat_ssl WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid();
SHOW data_checksums;

lsblk  # LUKS mapper active
pgbackrest info

docs.title: Monitoring Encryption Health
docs.text:
-- Non-SSL remote sessions (should be zero)
SELECT pid, usename, client_addr
FROM pg_stat_activity a
LEFT JOIN pg_stat_ssl s USING (pid)
WHERE client_addr IS NOT NULL
  AND (s.ssl IS NULL OR s.ssl = false);

-- All SSL connections
SELECT version, cipher, count(*)
FROM pg_stat_ssl
WHERE ssl = true
GROUP BY version, cipher;

Alerts: - Any remote connection without SSL - Certificate expiry < 30 days - Backup repo without cipher enabled - Unencrypted EBS/RDS instance detected (config audit)

docs.title: Compliance Quick Map
docs.text: Standard Required methods PCI-DSS 4.0 TLS 1.2+, encrypted storage, encrypted backups, key management HIPAA TLS + at-rest encryption + access audit (pgAudit) SOC 2 Encryption + rotation policy + monitoring GDPR At-rest + transit for personal data; column encryption for special categories ISO 27001 Documented key lifecycle; LUKS/KMS; TLS

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• SSL/TLS Implementation

  • pg_hba.conf
  • pgAudit
  • pgBackRest

docs.title: Encryption — Overview
docs.text: All encryption options for PostgreSQL 18 — start with the Encryption Methods reference for comparison and decision matrix.

docs.title: Guides
